original: Beginning of the five-day decisive Battle at Yarmuk: Muslim forces defeat the Byzantine army to take control of Syria for the first time
The Saracens conquer and destroy Taranto (southern Italy)
The cave city of Vardzia is consecrated by Queen Tamar of Georgia
Philip II of France marries Ingeborg, daughter of King Valdemar I of Denmark, in Amiens and requests an annulment immediately after on the grounds of non-consummation; the bride disputes
Güyük (Kuyuk), son and successor of Ogodei, formally elected Great Khan of the Mongols at Sira Ordu, five years after the death of his father
Construction of the new Cologne Cathedral in the Gothic style begins with the laying of the foundation stone by Archbishop Konrad von Hochstaden (completed 1880)
Knights of St. John conquer Rhodes
Malatestiana Library, the first humanist civic library in Europe, opens in association with the Convent of San Francesco in Cesena, Italy
"Mainz Psalter," the earliest dated book and the first to be printed in three colors, is completed
Sistine Chapel to Our Lady of the Assumption in Vatican City is consecrated with a Mass by Pope Sixtus IV [some sources cite August 9]
Seven Portuguese armed vessels led by Fernão Pires de Andrade meet Chinese officials at the Pearl River estuary
Panama City is founded by Spanish conquistador Pedro Arias Dávila
Ignatius of Loyola co-founds the Society of Jesus (Jesuit) religious order at Sanit Densi Church in Montmartre, France
Asunción is founded in Paraguay
Arequipa is founded in Peru
Mary, Queen of Scots, arrives in France at the age of 6
Nine Years' War: Battle of Curlew Pass - Irish forces led by Hugh Roe O'Donnell successfully ambush English forces led by Sir Conyers Clifford, who are sent to relieve Collooney Castle
First recorded hurricane in North America hits the Plymouth Colony
Dutch ship "Sperwer" strands on Jeju, Korea
France, Sweden, Bavaria, Brunswick, Münster, and Hesse-Kassel form the Confederation of the Rhine
Confederation of the Rhine disbands
Spain, Germany, and France sign the Truce of Ratisbon, ending the War of the Reunions; it is meant to last 20 years, but it lasts only four
United Lutheran Church in the American colonies is organized
Battle of Liegnitz: Prussian forces defeat Austrian and Russian armies
French Cardinal de Rohan is arrested in "The Affair of the Diamond Necklace"
Freed American slaves establish Liberia on the West African coast through the American Colonization Society (ACS)

Race riots in Cincinnati, Ohio, are spurred by the city's threat to enforce restrictive residency requirements on Black residents; over 1,000 people (more than half of the Black population) leave, many bound for Canada
Gregory XVI issues encyclical on liberalism and religious indifferentism, titled "Mirari Vos"
National Black Convention meets in Buffalo, New York
Tivoli Gardens, the world's second-oldest amusement park, opens in Copenhagen, Denmark
M. Waldo Hanchett patents the dental chair
Regular US mail to the Pacific coast begins
Confederate submarine "CSS H.L. Hunley" arrives in Charleston on railroad cars from Mobile, Alabama, where it is built
The Anglo-Satsuma War begins between the Satsuma Domain of Japan and the United Kingdom (Traditional Japanese date: July 2, 1863)
US Civil War: CSS Tallahassee captures six Yankee schooners off the New England coast
Battle of Lone Jack, Missouri: Fierce fighting between Union soldiers and Confederate forces, prompted by bushwhacker attacks, destroys the town of Lone Jack
The Representation of the People Act (1867) extends suffrage in the UK
The Meiji government in Japan establishes six new ministries, including one for Shinto
Transcontinental Railway is actually completed in Colorado
US law removes Native Americans from Black Hills after discovering gold
American MLB pitcher Guy Hecker scores 7 runs in 1 game
San Sebastian Church in Manila, the first all-steel church in Asia, is officially inaugurated and blessed
The US is no longer allowed exclusive rights in the Bering Sea
Louisville's pitcher Henry Dowling struck out 5 times in a game
Russian troops advance further into Manchuria
Arch Rock, a danger to San Francisco Bay shipping, is blasted with 30 tons of nitroglycerin
Great Britain issues a proclamation calling on the Boers to surrender by 15 September or face banishment and confiscation of their property
New Zealand's All Blacks play their first rugby test match against Australia's Wallabies at the Sydney Cricket Ground, the All Blacks win 22-3
Philadelphia A's future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcher Rube Waddell no-hits the St. Louis Browns, 2-0, in 5 innings
First freight delivery tunnel system begins operation underneath Chicago
Procter & Gamble unveils its Crisco shortening
Yankee Guy Zinn sets a record by stealing home twice in a game
Japan joins the Allies
Panama Canal opens under budget with the SS Ancon making the first official steamship passage through the canal
The first large public gathering of Boers in South Africa who do not want to support Britain in a war against Germany; British authorities try to repress this movement, but discontent spreads
Journalist Albert Siegfried Bettelheim is convicted of murder in Georgia
"The Sinking of the Lusitania" by Winsor McCay is produced; at 12 minutes long, it is the longest work of animation at that time
Mexico and the US reach an  accord over the oil concession of 1917
Norway annexes Spitsbergen
Dutch Spakenburg football team forms
Richard Grant and Ernest Lassy complete the longest canoe journey without portages or any aid (6,102 miles; 9,820 km)
Roy Wilkins joins the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) as Assistant Secretary
US ends occupation of Haiti after 19 years, which started after the 1915 assassination of Haiti's president
Americans make a clean sweep of the 3m diving medals with Marjorie Gestring, Katherine Rawls, and Dorothy Poynton-Hill at the Berlin Olympics
Annibale Frossi scores twice as Italy beats Austria 2-1 in extra time in the football final at the Berlin Olympics
Marshall Wayne and Elbert Root achieve an American 1-2 finish in the 10 m platform diving at the Berlin Olympics
The Berlin Olympic fencing competition concludes with Italy winning 4 of the 7 gold medals and Hungary taking the other 3
First night game at baseball's Comiskey Park in Chicago; White Sox beat St. Louis Browns 5-2
Thirteen Nazi Luftwaffe Stuka dive bombers crash into the ground during a disastrous practice demonstration at Neuhammer-am-Queis, Silesia, Germany (now Świętoszów, Poland) with no survivors
First edition of the Jewish Weekly newspaper in Amsterdam under Nazi occupation
Heavy dogfights over England: 75 German aircraft are damaged
Kovno Lithuanian Jews are herded into the Slobodka Ghetto
Five hostages are executed by Nazis in Sint-Michielsgestel
Allies land on Kiska, Aleutian Islands
Allied air raid on train in northern Netherlands kills 32 people
Operation Anvil: Allies land on French Mediterranean Sea coast
Operation Dragoon: Allied troops land in Provence
US 12th Army Corps enters Le Mans through Orléans
US 7th Armored Division reaches Chartres
A riot ensues in San Francisco as the city celebrates the end of WWII
US wartime rationing of gasoline and fuel oil ends
World War II: Korean Liberation Day
DuMont Television Network (WABD NY) launches in the US
GLEEP (Graphite Low Energy Experimental Pile), the first experimental nuclear reactor in Western Europe, begins operation in Oxford, England
India gains independence from the United Kingdom and remains a dominion until 1950
Republic of Korea (South Korea) is proclaimed (National Day)
WOTV TV channel 8 in Grand Rapids, MI (NBC) begins broadcasting
8.6 Assam-Tibet earthquake kills several thousand people
Indians make their first triple play at Cleveland Stadium
Joseph Pholien becomes Belgian Prime Minister
Rotterdam dock strike begins
Srikakulam district is formed in Andhra Pradesh, India
19th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Los Angeles 10, All-Stars 7 (88,316 attendees)
Nine inches of rainfall create a 20 ft (6 m) wave in Lynmouth, England, killing 34 people
WCHS TV channel 8 in Charleston-Huntington, WV (ABC) begins broadcasting
Hurricane Connie dissipates after killing 77 people on the US Eastern Seaboard and Mid-Atlantic states
WXEX TV channel 8 in Richmond-Petersburg, VA (ABC) begins broadcasting
David Simons reaches 30,942 meters in the Manhigh II balloon
USAF Captain Joe B. Jordan reaches 31,513 meters in an F-104 jet fighter
25th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: All-Stars 35, Detroit 19 (70,000 attendees)
Marshal Nikolai Bulganin resigns as Chairman of the Board of the State Bank of the USSR
A UFO is sighted by three California patrolmen
CFL's Calgary Stampeders move into McMahon Stadium
Chicago Bears beat NY Giants 16-7 in Toronto (NFL exhibition game)
Republic of the Congo (formerly Congo-Brazzaville) declares independence from France
Keiyo Road is specified as the first expressway in Japan
Netherlands and Indonesia sign accord over New Guinea
Shady Grove Baptist Church burned in Leesburg, Georgia
Fulbert Youlou resigns as President of Congo-Brazzaville
Fred Trueman takes his 300th Test cricket wicket (Neil Hawke)
MLB Philadelphia Phillies turn a triple play in an 8-1 victory over the Mets at Shea Stadium, New York City
Race riot in Dixmoor, Illinois, a suburb of Chicago
WEWS-TV in Cleveland, Ohio first airs "The Big 5 Show", a teen dance and music program later syndicated as "Upbeat"
Puerto Rican champion José Torres defeats American Eddie Cotton on points in Las Vegas to retain the WBC/WBA light-heavyweight boxing title
Radio Free Asia (South Korea) begins broadcasting
UK's Marine Offences Bill, which makes pirate radio stations a crime, goes into effect; pirate station Radio 355 closes down
Pirate Radio Free London begins transmitting
USSR performs a nuclear test in Sary Shagan, Soviet Union
Patricia Palinkas becomes the first woman professional football player (Orlando)
Bahrain gains independence from the United Kingdom
Charles Lismont wins the Helsinki marathon in 2:13:09.00
KVRL (now KRIV) TV ch 26 in Houston, Texas (Fox) begins broadcasting
The Social Democratic and Labour Party announces a campaign of civil disobedience in response to the introduction of internment in Northern Ireland
Black September kills 3 and wounds 55 in Athens
David Storey's play "Cromwell" premieres in London
USSR performs a nuclear test
France performs a nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Hurricane and floods ravage Bangladesh, killing 4,000 people
South Korean President Park Chung-Hee escapes assassination attempt
Joanne Little is acquitted of murdering her prison guard in North Carolina, becoming the first woman to successfully use the defense of deadly force to resist rape
Miki Takeo makes the first official pilgrimage to Yasukuni Shrine by an incumbent prime minister on the anniversary of the end of World War II
A possible alien radio signal from deep space, later dubbed the "Wow!" signal, is received at Big Ear, Ohio State's radio telescope in Delaware
Herbert Kappler, head of Nazi police and security services (SS) in Rome during WWII, escapes from a prison hospital in Rome
US House of Representatives approves (233-169) a 39-month extension for ERA
Andrew Young resigns as US ambassador to the United Nations
Robin Leamy of the US swims a record 7.98 kph for 50 m
Equatorial Guinea adopts a constitution
The PKK in Turkey starts a campaign of armed attacks against the Turkish military
First Iraqi air raid on Iran's main oil export terminal, Kharg Island
At the Pan Am Games in Indianapolis, USA and Cuba are tied with 2 outs in the 9th inning when Ty Griffin hits a home run, resulting in Cuba's first loss in 20 years of Pan Am competition
At 4 pm, LILCO consumers use a record 3,813 megawatts
New York City begins $70 million program to rebuild 900 Bronx apartments
Italian swimmer Giorgio Lamberti sets a world record in the 200 m freestyle (1:46.69) in Bonn, West Germany
At least 150 people die in clashes between the African National Congress and the Inkatha Freedom Party in South Africa
Mark McGwireis first to hit 30 home runs in each of his first 4 seasons
Phillies' Terry Mulholland no-hits Giants 6-0 (8th no-hitter of 1990)
US performs a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Colombo '92 closes in Genoa, Italy

Striker Brian Deane scores both goals for Sheffield United in a season-opening 2-1 home win over Manchester United at Bramall Lane; the first of Deane's goals is the first in the EPL's inaugural season
NYC radio (WFAN) personalityDon Imus's lung collapses for the second time
Televangelist Robert Tilton announces he is divorcing Marte
South African PresidentNelson Mandelareceives the Anne Frank Penning
"Macarena" single is released by Los del Río
Dan Wilson hits Seattle Mariners' 3,000th home run
Dow Jones Industrial Average drops 247.37 points
Battle of Roncevaux Pass: Roland, commander of the rearguard ofCharlemagne's army, is defeated by the Basques
Empire of Trebizond surrenders to forces ofSultan Mehmed II, the last remnant of the Byzantine Empire to fall; Emperor David is exiled and later executed
Louis XIis crowned King of France at Reims Cathedral after the death of his father, Charles VII
Jesuit priest SaintFrancis Xaviercomes ashore at Kagoshima (Traditional Japanese date: July 22, 1549)
Austrian composerJoseph Haydnleaves England forever, calling the time spent there "the happiest of his life"
Abraham Lincolnorders Union reinforcements be sent to Missouri
Fourth and last British government ofWilliam Gladstoneforms
ChineseEmpress Dowager Cixi, her family, and court retainers flee Peking for Xi'an as foreign troops move through the capital in an attempt to quell the Boxer Rebellion
KingEdward VIIof Great Britain visits German EmperorWilhelm IIto discuss the escalating rivalry between their nations' naval forces
Dinant, Belgium, is destroyed by German bombs, injuring Lt.Charles de Gaulle(24)

Taliesin Massacre (Spring Green, Wisconsin): Julian Carlton, a disgruntled employee, kills architectFrank Lloyd Wright's mistress Mamah Borthwick, her two children, and four others with a hatchet and burns the architect's home to the ground while Wright is away on business
US Secretary of StateWilliam Jennings Bryan, in a letter to J P Morgan, Jr., declares that loans to any of the belligerents go against US neutrality
PoetsWilfred Owenand Siegfried Sassoon first meet at Craiglockhart War Hospital, Edinburgh, and go on to have a profound influence on each other's work
Polish troops commanded byJózef Piłsudskidefeat the Soviets at the Battle of Warsaw (Miracle upon the Vistula)
Éamon de Valerais arrested in the Irish Free State
German Field MarshalGünther von Klugevanishes for one day; he kills himself on the 19th in the aftermath of the attempt to assassinate Adolf Hitler
Ezzard CharlesTKO's Freddie Beshore in the 14th round to retain the heavyweight boxing title
Sukarnoproclaims the Unitary Republic of Indonesia and becomes its first President
US GeneralOmar Bradleyleaves office as the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff after four years and retires from active military service
DictatorAlfredo Stroessnernames himself the 42nd President of Paraguay
American long jumperRalph Bostonsets a world record of 27' 3½" (8.31 m) in Kingston, Jamaica
Mayor Richard J. Daley declares "Ernie BanksDay" in Chicago, honoring the Cubs baseball player
Japanese community of San Francisco holdsMasanori MurakamiDay at Candlestick Park to honor the first Japanese player to play in the major leagues
The Beatles play to their largest crowd of over 55,000 at Shea Stadium, New York City; among those attending are future wivesBarbara BachandLinda Eastman
PopePaul VIpublishes constitution Regimini Ecclesiae Universae
Romanian President Nicolae Ceaușescu visits Prague

Despite crashing out on lap 36, ScotsmanJackie Stewartclinches his second Formula 1 World Drivers' Championship at the Austrian Grand Prix at Österreichring; Swiss driver Jo Siffert of the BRM team wins the race
US PresidentRichard Nixonannounces a 90-day freeze on wages, prices, and rents
Army officers in Bangladesh stage a military coup d'état, assassinate elected PresidentSheikh Mujibur Rahmanand most members of his family, and install Khondaker Mostaq Ahmad as President
Indian action-adventure film "Sholay," directed by Ramesh Sippy and starring Dharmendra andAmitabh Bachchan, is released as the highest-grossing Indian film ever
MLB Baltimore Orioles managerEarl Weaveris ejected from both games of a home doubleheader against the Texas Rangers
Apocalypse Now, directed byFrancis Ford Coppolaand starringMarlon Brando,Robert Duvall, andMartin Sheen, is released (Palme d'Or 1979)
P. W. Bothagives the "Rubicon" Speech in Durban, South Africa, disappointing many by refusing to consider immediate and major reforms to the country's apartheid system
US PresidentRonald Reagandecides to support a replacement for Challenger
F. W. de Klerkbecomes President of South Africa
In his second start since cancer treatment, Giants' Dave Dravecky breaks his pitching arm while throwing toTim Raines
Paul Simonperforms a free concert in Central Park, New York City
Nolan Ryanachieves his 324th and final victory as the Rangers defeat the Indians 4-1
Los Angeles Dodgers retire managerTommy Lasorda's #2
The Mayflower sets sail from Southampton, England, with 102 Pilgrims bound for the New World
Carla de Vries, an American tourist at the swimming event of the Berlin Olympics, findsAdolf Hitler"so friendly and gracious" that she shakes his hand and gives him a kiss

The Wizard of Oz, an American musical fantasy film adaptation of L. Frank Baum's book, premieres at Grauman's Chinese Theatre in Hollywood; directed by Victor Fleming and King Vidor with a cast featuring Judy Garland (Dorothy), Ray Bolger (Scarecrow), Jack Haley (Tin Man), Bert Lahr (Cowardly Lion), Frank Morgan (Wizard), Billie Burke (Glinda), andMargaret Hamilton(Wicked Witch); songs by Harold Arlen and Yip Harburg
Victory over Japan Day, surrender of Japan, signifies the end of World War II (August 14 in the Americas due to the time zone difference)
Woodstock Festival opens in Bethel, New York, on Max Yasgur's dairy farm; performers include Richie Havens, Tim Hardin,Ravi Shankar, Melanie, Arlo Guthrie, and Joan Baez
Alfonso IX, King of León and Galicia (1188-1230), born in Zamora (d. 1230)
Antony of Padua [Fernando Martins de Bulhões], Portuguese Catholic friar and saint (patron saint of Portugal), born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1231)
Luigi Pulci, Italian poet (Morgante), born in Florence (d. 1484)
Bartol Kašić, Croatian writer and linguist, born in Pag, Croatia (d. 1650)
Gilles Ménage, French scholar, born in Angers, France (d. 1692)
Hubertus Quellinus, Flemish cartoonist and painter, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1687)
Francesco Zuccarelli, Italian rococo painter and etcher, born in Pitigliano, Italy (d. 1788)
Blind Jack [John Metcalf], English 1st professional roadbuilder, born in Knaresborough, Yorkshire, England (d. 1810)
Ferdinando Bertoni, Italian composer, born in Salò, Lombardy, Republic of Venice (d. 1813)

Thomas Norris, English singer and composer, baptized in Mere, Wiltshire, England (d. 1790)

Marie-Anne Gaboury, French-Canadian settler (first white woman to settle in Western Canada), born in Maskinongé, Quebec (d. 1875)

Alexander Aliabiev, Russian composer remembered as one of the fathers of Russian art song, born in Tobolsk, Siberia, Russian Empire (d. 1851)

Elias M. Fries, Swedish botanist (System mycologicum), born in Femsjö, Sweden (d. 1878)
Sangolli Rayanna, Indian military chief warrior who led resistance against British East India Company annexation, born in Sangolli, Belagavi (d. 1831)
James Douglas, British-Canadian fur trader and politician (1st Governor of British Columbia 1858-64) called "The Father of British Columbia", born in Demerara, Guyana (d. 1877)
Charles Fisher, Canadian politician and jurist (Premier of the Colony of New Brunswick 1854-56, 1857-51), born in Fredericton, New Brunswick (d. 1880) [or born 16 Sept][1]
Louise Colet, French Romantic poet who had an affair with Gustave Flaubert, born in Aix-en-Provence, France (d. 1876)
Jules Grévy, 2nd President of the French Third Republic, born in Mont-sous-Vaudrey, France (d. 1891)
Virginia Clemm Poe, American wife of writer Edgar Allan Poe, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1847)[1]
Wilhelm Rust, German musicologist and composer, born in Dessau, Duchy of Anhalt-Dessau, German Confederation (d. 1892)
Orris Sanford Ferry, American lawyer, politician and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Bethel, Connecticut (d. 1875)
Eduard de Hartog, Dutch composer, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1909)
Hugh Archibald Clarke, Canadian organist, composer, and educator (The Music of The Spheres), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1927)
Walter Crane, English painter, cartoonist and illustrator (The Lady of Shalott), born in Liverpool (d. 1915)
Helena Amelung [Georgette Amelung], Dutch actress (Small Lord; De Spaansche Brabander), born in Amsterdam, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1906)

Albert Ballin, German inventor and shipping tycoon, born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1918)
Edith Nesbit, British children books author (The Story of the Treasure Seekers, Five Children and It), born in London, England (d. 1924)
Florence Harding [DeWolfe], US 1st lady (1921-23), born in Marion, Ohio (d. 1924)
Roosje Vos, Dutch seamstress, and trade union leader, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1932)

Johan Bernard Schepers, Frisian writer (Braga), born in Heerenveen, Netherlands (d. 1937)

Josef Klaus, Austrian conservative politician (Minister of Finance, 1961-63; Chancellor, 1966-70), born in Kötschach-Mauthen, Austria (d. 2001)
Leslie Coffelt, American White House Police officer, killed in the line of duty during an assassination attempt on Harry Truman, born in Oranda, Virginia (d. 1950)[1]

(Henry) "Huntz" Hall, American radio, stage, and screen actor (Dead End Kids: The Bowery Boys; Gas Pump Girls), born in New York City (d. 1999)

Gertrude Shope, South African leader of the ANC Women’s League, born in Johannesburg, South Africa (d. 2025)[1]
Mike Connors [Krekor Ohanian], American actor (Mannix), born in Fresno, California (d. 2017)
